symbols.name: y_min
symbols.def: Known finite lower endpoint of the outcome support.

symbols.name: y_max
symbols.def: Known finite upper endpoint of the outcome support.

symbols.name: X_space
symbols.def: Support of the pretreatment covariate vector X.

symbols.name: O
symbols.space: {(Y,S,D,X): Y in [y_min,y_max], S in {0,1}, D in supp(D), X in X_space}
symbols.def: Observed unit O = (Y,S,D,X).

symbols.name: O_i
symbols.space: {(Y,S,D,X): Y in [y_min,y_max], S in {0,1}, D in supp(D), X in X_space}
symbols.def: One observed replicate of O in the sample.

symbols.name: Y
symbols.def: Observed outcome component of O.

symbols.name: S
symbols.def: Observed selection indicator component of O.

symbols.name: D
symbols.def: Observed continuous or multivalued treatment component of O.

symbols.name: X
symbols.def: Observed pretreatment covariate vector.

symbols.name: P
symbols.def: Observed-data law of O under the Lee-Liu sufficient-set sample-selection model.

symbols.name: P_X
symbols.def: Marginal law of X induced by P.

symbols.name: d
symbols.def: Fixed interior treatment value at which the always-takers ADRF is evaluated.

symbols.name: a
symbols.def: Generic treatment value used to state local smoothness in the treatment coordinate around d.

symbols.name: Y(d)
symbols.def: Potential outcome under treatment value d.

symbols.name: S(d)
symbols.def: Potential selection indicator under treatment value d.

symbols.name: AT
symbols.def: Always-taker indicator for the Lee-Liu sufficient-set principal stratum anchored at dose d.

symbols.name: n
symbols.def: Observed sample size.

symbols.name: h_n
symbols.def: Kernel localization bandwidth at sample size n for target dose d.

symbols.name: r_n
symbols.def: Common nuisance-rate sequence used in the regular-regime expansion.

symbols.name: K
symbols.def: Kernel used to localize observations around the target dose d.

symbols.name: s_a
symbols.def: Selected mass family s_a(x) = P(S=1 | D=a, X=x).

symbols.name: f_a
symbols.def: Conditional density family f_a(x) = f_{D|X}(a|x).

symbols.name: Q_a
symbols.def: Conditional quantile family of Y given S=1, D=a, X=x.

symbols.name: s_d
symbols.def: Selected mass at dose d, s_d(x) = s_a(x) evaluated at a = d.

symbols.name: f_d
symbols.def: Conditional density of D at the target dose, f_d(x) = f_a(x) evaluated at a = d.

symbols.name: Q_d
symbols.def: Conditional quantile function of Y given S=1, D=d, X=x, equal to Q_a(u,x) evaluated at a = d.

symbols.name: underline_pi_d
symbols.def: Lee-Liu lower Frechet bound for the conditional always-taker mass at dose d.

symbols.name: overline_pi_d
symbols.def: Lee-Liu upper Frechet bound for the conditional always-taker mass at dose d.

symbols.name: pi
symbols.def: Candidate conditional always-taker mass selector.

symbols.name: Psi_d^L
symbols.def: Lower-tail trimmed numerator contribution at dose d.

symbols.name: Psi_d^U
symbols.def: Upper-tail trimmed numerator contribution at dose d.

symbols.name: Pi_d(P)
symbols.def: Feasible class of measurable selector functions compatible with the Frechet band at dose d.

symbols.name: sigma
symbols.def: Support-function direction indexing lower and upper endpoint problems.

symbols.name: N_sigma
symbols.def: Directional numerator map with N_{+1}(x,t) = Psi_d^U(x,t) and N_{-1}(x,t) = -Psi_d^L(x,t).

symbols.name: mu_AT(d)
symbols.def: Always-takers mean potential outcome at dose d, E[Y(d) | AT=1].

symbols.name: Theta_d(P)
symbols.def: Sharp identified set for mu_AT(d) under the sufficient-set model.

symbols.name: H_sigma(d;P)
symbols.def: Support-function value of Theta_d(P) in direction sigma.

symbols.name: lambda
symbols.def: Scalar argument of the Dinkelbach transform.

symbols.name: Phi_sigma(lambda;P)
symbols.def: Dinkelbach transform for the sigma-direction endpoint problem at dose d.

symbols.name: pi_{sigma,lambda}
symbols.def: Measurable selector attaining the pointwise transformed optimum argmax_{t in [underline_pi_d(X), overline_pi_d(X)]} {N_sigma(X,t) - lambda t}.

symbols.name: m_{sigma,lambda}
symbols.def: Conditional transformed value m_{sigma,lambda}(x) = sup_{t in [underline_pi_d(x), overline_pi_d(x)]} {N_sigma(x,t) - lambda t}.

symbols.name: Z_{sigma,n}(O,t;P)
symbols.def: Finite-bandwidth kernel-localized doubly robust signal at sample size n for the sigma-direction trimmed numerator contribution.

symbols.name: psi_{sigma,lambda,n}(O;P)
symbols.def: Finite-bandwidth orthogonal score for Phi_sigma(lambda;P) obtained by evaluating the localized trimmed signal at the transformed argmax selector and subtracting the population transformed numerator.

symbols.name: B_{sigma,lambda,n}(P)
symbols.def: Finite-bandwidth bias B_{sigma,lambda,n}(P) = E[psi_{sigma,lambda,n}(O;P)] - Phi_sigma(lambda;P).

symbols.name: lambda_sigma^*
symbols.def: Unique root of Phi_sigma(lambda;P) = 0 in direction sigma.

symbols.name: dot_Phi_sigma(P)
symbols.def: Population derivative partial_lambda Phi_sigma(lambda;P) evaluated at lambda = lambda_sigma^*.

symbols.name: pi_sigma^*
symbols.def: Measurable selector attaining the sigma-direction transformed pointwise optimum at lambda_sigma^*.

symbols.name: pi_sigma^PF
symbols.def: Pointwise Frechet selector solving the local ratio problem t -> N_sigma(X,t) / t on the Lee-Liu band.

symbols.name: C_sigma(d;P)
symbols.def: Indicator that the pointwise ratio optimizer also solves a common-threshold transformed problem with one scalar lambda across X.

symbols.name: hat_Phi_{sigma,n}
symbols.def: Cross-fitted sample analog hat_Phi_{sigma,n}(lambda) obtained by averaging the plug-in version of psi_{sigma,lambda,n}(O;P) over i = 1,...,n.

symbols.name: hat_lambda_{sigma,n}
symbols.def: Approximate root of hat_Phi_{sigma,n}(lambda) = 0.

symbols.name: varphi_{sigma,n}(O;P)
symbols.def: Centered finite-bandwidth score varphi_{sigma,n}(O;P) = psi_{sigma,lambda_sigma^*,n}(O;P) - E[psi_{sigma,lambda_sigma^*,n}(O;P)].

symbols.name: V_sigma(P)
symbols.def: Asymptotic variance constant V_sigma(P) = lim_{n -> infinity} h_n Var[varphi_{sigma,n}(O;P)] when the limit exists.

assumptions.condition: y_min < y_max and P(y_min <= Y <= y_max) = 1.
underline_pi_d(X) <= P(AT=1 | X) <= overline_pi_d(X) for P_X-almost every X.
0 < underline_pi_d(X) <= overline_pi_d(X) < s_d(X) for P_X-almost every X.
There exist constants 0 < c_f < C_f < infinity such that c_f <= f_d(X) <= C_f for P_X-almost every X.

standard.name: positive local treatment density at the target dose

assumptions.condition: There exists delta_d > 0 such that, for P_X-almost every X and every u in [underline_pi_d(X) / s_d(X), 1 - overline_pi_d(X) / s_d(X)], the maps a -> s_a(X), a -> f_a(X), and a -> Q_a(u,X) are twice continuously differentiable on [d - delta_d, d + delta_d] intersect supp(D), with second derivatives uniformly bounded in absolute value by a finite constant.

standard.name: second-order local smoothness in the treatment coordinate

assumptions.condition: For P_X-almost every x, the conditional law of Y given S=1, D=d, X=x has a density on [y_min,y_max] that is continuous and bounded away from 0 on neighborhoods of Q_d(underline_pi_d(x) / s_d(x), x) and Q_d(1 - overline_pi_d(x) / s_d(x), x).

underline_pi_d and overline_pi_d are P_X-measurable, and for each sigma the map (x,t) -> N_sigma(x,t) is jointly measurable and continuous in t on [underline_pi_d(x),overline_pi_d(x)]; for every lambda, the argmax correspondences of t -> N_sigma(x,t)-lambda t and t -> N_sigma(x,t)/t over that interval admit P_X-measurable selections.

novel.justification: standard: measurable maximum/selection regularity for compact-valued correspondences

assumptions.condition: The observations O_1,...,O_n are independent and identically distributed with common observed-data law P.

novel.justification: standard: iid sampling for triangular-array central limit theory

assumptions.condition: K is bounded, symmetric, compactly supported, integral K(u)du=1, integral u K(u)du=0, and integral u^2 |K(u)|du<infinity.

novel.justification: standard: second-order compactly supported kernel regularity for local constant continuous-treatment smoothing

assumptions.condition: h_n -> 0 and n h_n / log(1/h_n) -> infinity.

novel.justification: standard: uniform consistency bandwidth condition for compact-index kernel averages

assumptions.condition: For each sigma and compact Lambda_sigma, the oracle localized score class {psi_{sigma,lambda,n}(.;P): lambda in Lambda_sigma} has measurable lambda-continuous versions, a scalar Lipschitz modulus in lambda, envelope |psi_{sigma,lambda,n}(O;P)| <= C{1+|K((D-d)/h_n)|/h_n}, variance sup_{lambda in Lambda_sigma} Var_P(psi_{sigma,lambda,n}(O;P)) <= C/h_n, and a residual conditional mean that is continuous at D=d uniformly in lambda.

novel.justification: standard: compact-parametric kernel empirical-process regularity and approximate-identity continuity

definitions.name: Trimmed numerator contributions
definitions.construction: Psi_d^L(x,t) = s_d(x) * integral_0^{t / s_d(x)} Q_d(u,x) du and Psi_d^U(x,t) = s_d(x) * integral_{1 - t / s_d(x)}^1 Q_d(u,x) du for x in X_space and t in [underline_pi_d(x), overline_pi_d(x)].

definitions.name: Feasible selector class
definitions.construction: Pi_d(P) = {pi : X_space -> [0,1] : pi is measurable and underline_pi_d(x) <= pi(x) <= overline_pi_d(x) for P_X-almost every x}.

definitions.name: Global support function
definitions.construction: H_sigma(d;P) = sup_{pi in Pi_d(P)} E[N_sigma(X, pi(X))] / E[pi(X)].

definitions.name: Dinkelbach transform
definitions.construction: Phi_sigma(lambda;P) = E[sup_{t in [underline_pi_d(X), overline_pi_d(X)]} {N_sigma(X,t) - lambda t}].

definitions.name: Pointwise Frechet plug-in selector
definitions.construction: For P_X-almost every x, pi_sigma^PF(x) is any measurable selection from argmax_{t in [underline_pi_d(x), overline_pi_d(x)]} N_sigma(x,t) / t.

definitions.name: Common-threshold condition
definitions.construction: C_sigma(d;P) holds iff there exists lambda in R such that Phi_sigma(lambda;P) = 0 and, for P_X-almost every x, every t in argmax_{u in [underline_pi_d(x), overline_pi_d(x)]} N_sigma(x,u) / u also belongs to argmax_{u in [underline_pi_d(x), overline_pi_d(x)]} {N_sigma(x,u) - lambda u}.

definitions.name: Regular root derivative, finite-bandwidth bias, and variance objects
definitions.construction: Define the unlocalized trimmed bracket G_sigma(O,t;P) so that E[G_sigma(O,t;P)|D=d,X=x]=N_sigma(x,t). Set the localized residual signal to Z^{res}_{sigma,n}(O,t;P)=N_sigma(X,t)+K((D-d)/h_n){G_sigma(O,t;P)-N_sigma(X,t)}/(h_n f_d(X)), and define psi_{sigma,lambda,n}(O;P)=m_{sigma,lambda}(X)+Z^{res}_{sigma,n}(O,pi_{sigma,lambda}(X);P)-N_sigma(X,pi_{sigma,lambda}(X)). Equivalently, psi=m_{sigma,lambda}(X)+K((D-d)/h_n){G_sigma(O,pi_{sigma,lambda}(X);P)-N_sigma(X,pi_{sigma,lambda}(X))}/(h_n f_d(X)). Keep B, dot_Phi, varphi, and V defined from this corrected psi.

definitions.name: Kernel-localized doubly robust endpoint handle
definitions.construction: For the proved regular endpoint module, use the oracle known-nuisance transform hat_Phi^or_{sigma,n}(lambda)=P_n psi_{sigma,lambda,n}(O;P), where psi is the population-nuisance score from def:regular-root-objects and all population nuisance functions and Frechet-band endpoints are held fixed. Let a_n=(n h_n)^(1/2), let B_{sigma,lambda,n}(P)=E_P psi_{sigma,lambda,n}(O;P)-Phi_sigma(lambda;P), and choose an oracle approximate root hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n} in Lambda_sigma satisfying a_n |hat_Phi^or_{sigma,n}(hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n})|=o_P(1). Feasible estimated-nuisance transforms, active-set slope estimators, multiplier critical values, and boundary-uniform confidence sets are not part of this positive definition and are deferred to oeq:boundary-uniform-inference.

statements.statement: Under ass:bounded-outcome, ass:frechet-band-compatibility, and ass:selected-mass-overlap, the sharp identified set Theta_d(P) for mu_AT(d) is the interval [ -H_{-1}(d;P), H_{+1}(d;P) ]. For each sigma in {-1,+1}, H_sigma(d;P) equals the unique scalar lambda_sigma^* satisfying Phi_sigma(lambda_sigma^*;P) = 0, and there exists a measurable selector pi_sigma^* in Pi_d(P) such that pi_sigma^*(X) belongs to argmax_{t in [underline_pi_d(X), overline_pi_d(X)]} {N_sigma(X,t) - lambda_sigma^* t} for P_X-almost every X.
statements.justification: This is the theorem-level kernel that converts the deferred sufficient-set sharpness problem into a measurable-selection support function with a scalar threshold certificate.
statements.gap: LeeLiu2025ContinuousLee leaves the sufficient-set covariate case with conditional Frechet bands, and no cited paper gives the law-level global representation needed to aggregate those bands sharply when the denominator E[pi(X)] is unknown.
statements.consumer: It gives an exact endpoint formula that a later solver can compute or estimate without pretending that pointwise selector choices paste automatically into a globally sharp law.
statements.proof_tex: Let l(X)=underline_pi_d(X) and u(X)=overline_pi_d(X). By ass:selected-mass-overlap, 0<l(X)<=u(X)<s_d(X)<=1 P_X-a.e.; hence every pi in Pi_d(P) has E pi(X)>=E l(X)>0 and the trimming fractions pi(X)/s_d(X) are in [0,1]. Lemma lem:conditional-trimming-fiber-attainment gives the exact conditional feasible numerator fiber: conditional on X=x and on a feasible always-taker mass t in [l(x),u(x)], the conditional numerator q(x)=E[Y(d)1{AT=1}|X=x] ranges exactly over [Psi_d^L(x,t),Psi_d^U(x,t)]. Lemma lem:latent-completion-pasting pastes those conditional kernels measurably over x without changing the observed law P. Therefore lem:identified-set-ratio-envelope applies and gives the sharp identified set as the interval with lower endpoint inf_{pi in Pi_d(P)} E[Psi_d^L(X,pi(X))]/E[pi(X)] and upper endpoint sup_{pi in Pi_d(P)} E[Psi_d^U(X,pi(X))]/E[pi(X)]. Since N_{+1}=Psi_d^U and N_{-1}=-Psi_d^L, def:support-function identifies the upper endpoint as H_{+1}(d;P) and the lower endpoint as -H_{-1}(d;P). Thus Theta_d(P)=[-H_{-1}(d;P),H_{+1}(d;P)]. It remains to prove the scalar threshold certificate. Fix sigma. Lemma lem:selector-envelope-interchange gives Phi_sigma(lambda;P)=sup_{pi in Pi_d(P)} E[N_sigma(X,pi(X))-lambda pi(X)] and gives a measurable pointwise maximizer. Lemma lem:dinkelbach-root-certificate applies to the fractional program because the denominator is bounded below by E l(X)>0: H_sigma(d;P) is finite and is the unique scalar lambda satisfying Phi_sigma(lambda;P)=0. Taking lambda_sigma^*=H_sigma(d;P), the measurable maximizer supplied by lem:selector-envelope-interchange at lambda_sigma^* is a selector pi_sigma^* in Pi_d(P) and satisfies pi_sigma^*(X) in argmax_{t in [l(X),u(X)]}{N_sigma(X,t)-lambda_sigma^* t} P_X-a.e. This proves both the sharp identified interval and the global Dinkelbach representation.

statements.statement: Fix sigma in {-1,+1}. Under thm:global-support-function, if C_sigma(d;P) holds, then the pointwise Frechet plug-in is globally sharp in direction sigma: H_sigma(d;P) = E[N_sigma(X, pi_sigma^PF(X))] / E[pi_sigma^PF(X)]. Conversely, if pi_sigma^PF is globally sharp and the argmax set in def:pointwise-plugin is P_X-almost surely singleton, then C_sigma(d;P) holds.
statements.justification: The practical question is when the easy pointwise rule is already law-level sharp; this theorem names the exact primitive certificate.
statements.gap: JonzonGabrielSjolanderSachs2025CovariatesBounds distinguishes pointwise and uniform sharpness abstractly, but it does not provide a model-specific if-and-only-if threshold criterion for a continuous-treatment Lee problem.
statements.consumer: It tells an empirical analyst when global optimization is unnecessary and when the gap between pointwise and global sharpness is a real identification issue.
statements.proof_tex:
Fix sigma in {-1,+1}. Write l(x)=underline_pi_d(x), u(x)=overline_pi_d(x), and, for a scalar lambda, m_lambda(x)=sup_{t in [l(x),u(x)]}{N_sigma(x,t)-lambda t}. Under thm:global-support-function, H_sigma(d;P) is the unique zero of Phi_sigma(lambda;P)=E[m_lambda(X)], and the feasible denominators are strictly positive because every feasible selector is bounded below by l(X) and the selected-mass overlap used in thm:global-support-function gives E[l(X)]>0.

Forward direction. Suppose C_sigma(d;P) holds. By def:common-threshold-condition, there is a scalar lambda with Phi_sigma(lambda;P)=0 such that every pointwise ratio maximizer also maximizes t -> N_sigma(X,t)-lambda t over [l(X),u(X)] for P_X-a.e. X. The selector pi_sigma^PF is, by def:pointwise-plugin, a measurable pointwise ratio maximizer; hence N_sigma(X,pi_sigma^PF(X))-lambda pi_sigma^PF(X)=m_lambda(X) a.e. Taking expectations gives E[N_sigma(X,pi_sigma^PF(X))]-lambda E[pi_sigma^PF(X)]=E[m_lambda(X)]=Phi_sigma(lambda;P)=0. Since E[pi_sigma^PF(X)]>0, E[N_sigma(X,pi_sigma^PF(X))]/E[pi_sigma^PF(X)]=lambda. The zero of Phi_sigma is unique and equals H_sigma(d;P), again by thm:global-support-function, so lambda=H_sigma(d;P). Therefore H_sigma(d;P)=E[N_sigma(X,pi_sigma^PF(X))]/E[pi_sigma^PF(X)].

Converse. Suppose pi_sigma^PF is globally sharp and the pointwise argmax set in def:pointwise-plugin is P_X-a.s. singleton. Set lambda=H_sigma(d;P). Thm:global-support-function gives Phi_sigma(lambda;P)=0. It also gives the Dinkelbach support inequality at the root: for every feasible pi, E[N_sigma(X,pi(X))-lambda pi(X)]<=Phi_sigma(lambda;P)=0. Global sharpness of pi_sigma^PF means E[N_sigma(X,pi_sigma^PF(X))]/E[pi_sigma^PF(X)]=lambda, so E[N_sigma(X,pi_sigma^PF(X))-lambda pi_sigma^PF(X)]=0. Pointwise, N_sigma(X,pi_sigma^PF(X))-lambda pi_sigma^PF(X)<=m_lambda(X). Integrating and using E[m_lambda(X)]=Phi_sigma(lambda;P)=0 shows that the nonnegative gap m_lambda(X)-{N_sigma(X,pi_sigma^PF(X))-lambda pi_sigma^PF(X)} has expectation zero; hence it is zero P_X-a.e. Thus pi_sigma^PF(X) belongs to argmax_{t in [l(X),u(X)]}{N_sigma(X,t)-lambda t} for P_X-a.e. X. Since the ratio argmax set is singleton a.e. and pi_sigma^PF is its measurable selection, every t in argmax_{u in [l(X),u(X)]} N_sigma(X,u)/u is equal to pi_sigma^PF(X) a.e.; therefore every such t also belongs to the transformed argmax at the same lambda. Together with Phi_sigma(lambda;P)=0, this is exactly C_sigma(d;P) by def:common-threshold-condition.

statements.statement: If underline_pi_d(X) = overline_pi_d(X) for P_X-almost every X, then C_sigma(d;P) holds for sigma = -1 and sigma = +1, and Theta_d(P) collapses to the Lee-Liu sufficient-value functional obtained by plugging the identified always-taker mass into the trimmed selected-outcome formulas.
statements.justification: A correct proposal must reduce to the known sufficient-value case rather than quietly changing the target problem.
statements.gap: This is the required sanity reduction to LeeLiu2025ContinuousLee rather than a novelty claim.
statements.consumer: It gives the solver and the reviewer a clean benchmark special case where the new representation must agree with the published formula.
statements.proof_tex:
Assume underline_pi_d(X)=overline_pi_d(X) for P_X-a.e. X and put pi_0(X)=underline_pi_d(X)=overline_pi_d(X). Then, modulo P_X-null sets, Pi_d(P) contains only the selector pi_0. Consequently, for each sigma in {-1,+1}, both the local ratio problem t -> N_sigma(X,t)/t and the transformed local problem t -> N_sigma(X,t)-lambda t over [underline_pi_d(X),overline_pi_d(X)] have the same singleton feasible set {pi_0(X)} for every lambda. Taking lambda=H_sigma(d;P), the Dinkelbach root supplied by the support-function theorem invoked in thm:pointwise-plugin-sharpness, gives Phi_sigma(lambda;P)=0, and the singleton feasible-set observation verifies C_sigma(d;P) for sigma=-1 and sigma=+1.

Applying thm:pointwise-plugin-sharpness in the two directions gives H_{+1}(d;P)=E[Psi_d^U(X,pi_0(X))]/E[pi_0(X)] and H_{-1}(d;P)=E[-Psi_d^L(X,pi_0(X))]/E[pi_0(X)], because N_{+1}=Psi_d^U and N_{-1}=-Psi_d^L. The global support-function theorem identifies Theta_d(P)=[-H_{-1}(d;P),H_{+1}(d;P)]. Substituting the two displayed formulae yields Theta_d(P)=[E[Psi_d^L(X,pi_0(X))]/E[pi_0(X)], E[Psi_d^U(X,pi_0(X))]/E[pi_0(X)]]. This is exactly the Lee-Liu sufficient-value functional obtained by plugging the identified always-taker mass pi_0 into the lower- and upper-tail trimmed selected-outcome formulas.

statements.statement: Fix sigma in {-1,+1}. Let Lambda_sigma be a compact scalar search set containing lambda_sigma^*(P) in its interior. Work with the known-population-nuisance Dinkelbach sample transform hat_Phi^or_{sigma,n}(lambda)=P_n psi_{sigma,lambda,n}(O;P) from def:regular-root-objects and def:dr-endpoint-handle, and let hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n} in Lambda_sigma be any measurable approximate zero satisfying |hat_Phi^or_{sigma,n}(hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n})|=o_P(1) (equivalently, an o_P(1)-minimizer of lambda -> |hat_Phi^or_{sigma,n}(lambda)| over Lambda_sigma). Under thm:global-support-function, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:selected-mass-overlap, ass:local-dose-density, ass:local-dose-smoothness, ass:kernel-regularity, ass:iid-sampling, ass:measurable-maximum-regularity, ass:kernel-bandwidth-consistency, ass:oracle-transform-lambda-regularity, def:regular-root-objects, def:dr-endpoint-handle, lem:regular-envelope-derivative, and lem:uniform-transform-ulln, hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n} ->p lambda_sigma^*(P). Consequently, applying the result for sigma=-1 and sigma=+1, the estimated endpoint pair (-hat_lambda^or_{-1,n}, hat_lambda^or_{+1,n}) converges in probability in R^2 to (-lambda_{-1}^*(P), lambda_{+1}^*(P)); equivalently, -hat_lambda^or_{-1,n} and hat_lambda^or_{+1,n} converge to the lower and upper endpoints of Theta_d(P)=[-lambda_{-1}^*(P), lambda_{+1}^*(P)], respectively. No (n h_n)^(1/2) limit law, finite-bandwidth bias expansion, variance estimation, derivative estimation, studentization, Wald coverage, feasible estimated-nuisance linearization, boundary law, tie-law, or uniform coverage conclusion is claimed.
statements.justification: The identification result is materially more useful if the smooth-root submodel admits an endpoint estimator whose first-order law keeps the kernel bias explicit and uses the correct h_n-scaled variance normalization.
statements.gap: ColangeloLee2025ContinuousDML proves kernel-localized doubly robust inference for point-identified ADRFs, and BenMichael2025ConditionalLP treats expectation-of-conditional-LP bounds, but neither covers a denominator-coupled linear-fractional support function with an estimated selector root, explicit finite-bandwidth bias, and active-set-dependent variance scaling.
statements.consumer: It supplies the operational regular-regime endpoint estimator and Wald inference module that a later solver can turn into a concrete inferential procedure without conflating the finite-bandwidth score with its h_n -> 0 target.
statements.proof_tex: Fix sigma and write lambda^*=lambda_sigma^*(P), Lambda=Lambda_sigma, Phi(lambda)=Phi_sigma(lambda;P), and hatPhi_n(lambda)=hat_Phi^or_{sigma,n}(lambda). By thm:global-support-function, lambda^* is the unique scalar root of Phi(lambda)=0 and Theta_d(P)=[-lambda_{-1}^*(P),lambda_{+1}^*(P)]. By lem:regular-envelope-derivative, Phi is continuous on Lambda and its root derivative at lambda^* is strictly negative; in particular the unique zero is locally non-flat. Continuity and uniqueness on the compact set Lambda imply global separation on Lambda: for each epsilon>0, A_epsilon={lambda in Lambda: |lambda-lambda^*|>=epsilon} is compact and contains no zero of Phi, hence eta_epsilon:=inf_{lambda in A_epsilon}|Phi(lambda)| is strictly positive. Indeed, if eta_epsilon=0, a compactness subsequence lambda_k -> lambda_bar in A_epsilon with |Phi(lambda_k)| -> 0 would give Phi(lambda_bar)=0, contradicting uniqueness of lambda^*. By lem:uniform-transform-ulln, sup_{lambda in Lambda}|hatPhi_n(lambda)-Phi(lambda)| ->p 0 for the known-population-nuisance transform from def:regular-root-objects and def:dr-endpoint-handle. The approximate-zero condition gives |hatPhi_n(hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n})|=o_P(1). On the event sup_{lambda in Lambda}|hatPhi_n(lambda)-Phi(lambda)| < eta_epsilon/3 and |hatPhi_n(hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n})| < eta_epsilon/3, if |hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n}-lambda^*|>=epsilon then hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n} belongs to A_epsilon and therefore eta_epsilon <= |Phi(hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n})| <= |Phi(hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n})-hatPhi_n(hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n})|+|hatPhi_n(hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n})| < 2 eta_epsilon/3, a contradiction. Hence P(|hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n}-lambda^*|>=epsilon) tends to zero for every epsilon>0, so hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n} ->p lambda_sigma^*(P). Applying the same argument separately for sigma=-1 and sigma=+1 and using a union bound gives convergence in probability in R^2 of (-hat_lambda^or_{-1,n},hat_lambda^or_{+1,n}) to (-lambda_{-1}^*(P),lambda_{+1}^*(P)). By thm:global-support-function these two limits are exactly the lower and upper endpoints of the sharp interval Theta_d(P)=[-lambda_{-1}^*(P),lambda_{+1}^*(P)]. The argument used only uniform consistency and deterministic root separation; it does not assert an (n h_n)^(1/2) expansion, a finite-bandwidth bias expansion, variance or derivative estimation, studentization, Wald coverage, feasible nuisance linearization, boundary or tie-law behavior, or uniform coverage.

statements.statement: Open problem (oracle and feasible distributional inference beyond plug-in consistency). The positive results establish the sharp support-function/Dinkelbach identification formula and plug-in consistency of the Dinkelbach endpoint roots only. It remains open to derive the (n h_n)^(1/2) limit law for kernel-localized Dinkelbach endpoint roots, both in the oracle known-nuisance experiment and with feasible estimated nuisances; to keep the finite-bandwidth bias term explicit and prove when it is negligible; to justify feasible cross-fitted or debiased linearization of hat_Phi^feas_{sigma,n}, including the plug-in transform drift term rather than dropping it; to estimate the variance and the ordinary or directional root derivative; to prove the associated oracle score process and multiplier process converge jointly; to estimate active-set denominator slopes uniformly; and to construct Wald or multiplier confidence sets with pointwise, boundary, tie-law, and uniform coverage for Theta_d(P). None of the Gaussian expansion, feasible Wald inference, variance estimation, derivative estimation, studentization, boundary-directional inference, or uniform coverage theory is established by the positive results.
statements.justification: The hard statistical content is honest coverage when the selector hits a Frechet boundary or a tie law makes the root map only directionally differentiable.
statements.gap: FangSantos2019DirectionalInference gives the generic directional-delta template, but no current paper derives it for a kernel-localized Lee-type endpoint whose identified set comes from a denominator-coupled measurable-selector program.
statements.consumer: A positive answer upgrades regular Wald inference to honest identified-set inference that remains valid at threshold ties and boundary laws.
statements.proof_tex: For each sigma in {-1,+1}, prop:regular-dr-endpoint proves hat_lambda^or_{sigma,n} ->p lambda_sigma^*(P). Hence the ordered plug-in endpoint pair (-hat_lambda^or_{-1,n},hat_lambda^or_{+1,n}) converges in probability to (-lambda_{-1}^*(P),lambda_{+1}^*(P)), the lower and upper endpoints of the sharp identified interval supplied by thm:global-support-function. No Gaussian expansion, feasible Wald inference, variance estimation, derivative estimation, studentization, boundary-directional inference, or uniform coverage conclusion follows from the current positive results.

statements.statement: Under ass:bounded-outcome, ass:selected-mass-overlap, def:tail-integrals, def:feasible-selector-class, and def:dinkelbach-transform, for each sigma in {-1,+1} and lambda in R, sup_{pi in Pi_d(P)} E[N_sigma(X,pi(X))-lambda pi(X)] equals Phi_sigma(lambda;P), and there is a measurable selector pi_{sigma,lambda} in Pi_d(P) attaining the pointwise supremum P_X-a.e.
statements.proof_tex:
Fix sigma and lambda, and write I(x)=[underline_pi_d(x),overline_pi_d(x)] and f_lambda(x,t)=N_sigma(x,t)-lambda t. By ass:selected-mass-overlap, I(x) is nonempty and compact for P_X-a.e. x. The endpoint functions underline_pi_d and overline_pi_d are identified conditional-law functionals and hence are taken in their measurable versions; def:feasible-selector-class is formed from these measurable versions. By def:tail-integrals and ass:bounded-outcome, for fixed x the maps t -> Psi_d^L(x,t)=s_d(x) integral_0^{t/s_d(x)} Q_d(u,x)du and t -> Psi_d^U(x,t)=s_d(x) integral_{1-t/s_d(x)}^1 Q_d(u,x)du are continuous on I(x), because they are indefinite integrals of the bounded quantile function Q_d(.,x). Hence t -> f_lambda(x,t) is continuous on I(x) for sigma=+1 and sigma=-1.

Measurability of the value and of a selector can be obtained directly in this one-dimensional compact problem. Let Q_0 be the rationals in [0,1] and let q_I(x)=min(max(q,underline_pi_d(x)),overline_pi_d(x)). Continuity implies

m_lambda(x)=max_{t in I(x)} f_lambda(x,t)=sup_{q in Q_0} f_lambda(x,q_I(x)).

The right side is a countable supremum of measurable functions, so m_lambda is measurable. Define the lexicographically leftmost maximizer by

pi_{sigma,lambda}(x)=inf{t in I(x): f_lambda(x,t)=m_lambda(x)}.

For every real a, the event {pi_{sigma,lambda}(x)<a} can be written, up to null sets, as a countable union over rational q<a of the measurable events {q in I(x), f_lambda(x,q) >= m_lambda(x)-1/k for all k large}; equivalently one may take limits of rational 1/k-maximizers. Thus pi_{sigma,lambda} is measurable. It belongs to Pi_d(P) by def:feasible-selector-class and attains m_lambda(X) a.e.

For any pi in Pi_d(P), f_lambda(X,pi(X)) <= m_lambda(X) a.e., so

sup_{pi in Pi_d(P)} E[f_lambda(X,pi(X))] <= E[m_lambda(X)].

The measurable maximizer pi_{sigma,lambda} attains equality. By def:dinkelbach-transform, E[m_lambda(X)]=Phi_sigma(lambda;P). This proves the interchange and the existence of a feasible measurable pointwise maximizer.

statements.statement: For each sigma in {-1,+1} and lambda in R, the transformed selector envelope F_sigma(lambda)=sup_{pi in Pi_d(P)} E[N_sigma(X,pi(X))-lambda pi(X)] equals Phi_sigma(lambda;P), and the supremum is attained by a measurable pointwise maximizer in Pi_d(P).
statements.proof_tex: This is the notation-only consequence of lem:primitive-measurable-maximum-interchange. That lemma applies to f_lambda(x,t)=N_sigma(x,t)-lambda t on the compact feasible interval [underline_pi_d(x),overline_pi_d(x)], gives a feasible measurable maximizer pi_{sigma,lambda}, and shows that the supremum over measurable feasible selectors equals the expectation of the pointwise envelope. Def:dinkelbach-transform names that expectation Phi_sigma(lambda;P).

statements.statement: Under ass:bounded-outcome, ass:selected-mass-overlap, def:feasible-selector-class, def:support-function, and lem:selector-envelope-interchange, for each sigma in {-1,+1}, H_sigma(d;P) is finite and is the unique scalar lambda satisfying Phi_sigma(lambda;P)=0; at this lambda any measurable transformed maximizer with zero transformed value attains the ratio H_sigma(d;P).
statements.proof_tex: Let c_pi(P)=E underline_pi_d(X). Ass:selected-mass-overlap gives underline_pi_d(X)>0 a.e., hence c_pi(P)>0, and every pi in Pi_d(P) satisfies E pi(X)>=c_pi(P). Bounded outcomes imply |N_sigma(X,pi(X))| is bounded by a finite constant times pi(X), so all ratios in def:support-function are finite. Write R(pi)=E N_sigma(X,pi(X))/E pi(X) and H=sup_{pi in Pi_d(P)} R(pi). By lem:selector-envelope-interchange, Phi_sigma(lambda;P)=sup_pi E pi(X){R(pi)-lambda}. If lambda<H, choose pi with R(pi)>lambda; then Phi_sigma(lambda;P)>0. If lambda=H, all terms are <=0, while a sequence pi_k with R(pi_k) up to H gives E pi_k(X){R(pi_k)-H} approaching 0 from below because E pi_k(X)<=1; hence Phi_sigma(H;P)=0. If lambda>H, then R(pi)-lambda<=H-lambda for every pi, so E pi(X){R(pi)-lambda}<=(H-lambda)c_pi(P)<0, and Phi_sigma(lambda;P)<0. Thus H is the unique zero. If pi^* is a transformed maximizer at lambda=H and E[N_sigma(X,pi^*(X))-H pi^*(X)]=Phi_sigma(H;P)=0, then E pi^*(X)>0 and division gives R(pi^*)=H.

statements.statement: Under thm:global-support-function, ass:selected-mass-overlap, ass:measurable-maximum-regularity, def:feasible-selector-class, def:dinkelbach-transform, and def:regular-root-objects, Phi_sigma(lambda;P) is Lipschitz and continuous in lambda; at lambda_sigma^*(P), every directional envelope slope is the negative expected mass of an active transformed selector, so any ordinary derivative dot_Phi_sigma(P) equals -E[tilde pi_sigma(X)] for some active transformed maximizer and is strictly negative; consequently the zero lambda_sigma^*(P) is well separated on every compact Lambda_sigma containing it as the unique zero.
statements.proof_tex:
Fix sigma. For x and feasible t in [underline_pi_d(x),overline_pi_d(x)], the map lambda -> N_sigma(x,t)-lambda t is affine with slope -t. Since def:feasible-selector-class restricts t to [0,1], the pointwise envelope

 m_{sigma,lambda}(x)=sup_{t in [underline_pi_d(x),overline_pi_d(x)]}{N_sigma(x,t)-lambda t}

satisfies |m_{sigma,lambda}(x)-m_{sigma,lambda'}(x)| <= |lambda-lambda'| for every lambda,lambda'. Integrating and using def:dinkelbach-transform gives |Phi_sigma(lambda;P)-Phi_sigma(lambda';P)| <= |lambda-lambda'|. Hence Phi_sigma is Lipschitz and continuous.

Now fix lambda and let A_lambda(x) be the active transformed argmax correspondence. Ass:measurable-maximum-regularity gives measurable active selectors. For h>0 and any active selectors t_0(x) in A_lambda(x) and t_h(x) in A_{lambda+h}(x), the envelope inequalities give

 -h t_0(x) <= m_{sigma,lambda+h}(x)-m_{sigma,lambda}(x) <= -h t_h(x).

The analogous inequalities for h<0 reverse the roles of the two active sets. Since all selectors are bounded by 1, dominated convergence applies to any convergent active-selector branch. Thus every one-sided directional envelope slope at lambda is the negative expectation of a measurable active transformed selector, and if the ordinary derivative dot_Phi_sigma(P) exists at lambda_sigma^*(P), it equals -E[tilde pi_sigma(X)] for some active transformed maximizer tilde pi_sigma.

By ass:selected-mass-overlap, every feasible active selector obeys tilde pi_sigma(X)>=underline_pi_d(X)>0 P_X-a.e. Because tilde pi_sigma is bounded and nonnegative, E[tilde pi_sigma(X)]>0; hence dot_Phi_sigma(P)=-E[tilde pi_sigma(X)]<0. Finally, thm:global-support-function gives lambda_sigma^*(P) as the unique zero of Phi_sigma. A continuous function with a unique zero on a compact set is bounded away from zero outside every neighborhood of that zero: for every epsilon>0, the compact set {lambda in Lambda_sigma: |lambda-lambda_sigma^*(P)|>=epsilon} contains no zero, so the infimum of |Phi_sigma(lambda;P)| over it is strictly positive. This is the claimed well-separated-root property.

statements.statement: Fix sigma in {-1,+1} and a compact Lambda_sigma. Under ass:bounded-outcome, ass:local-dose-density, ass:local-dose-smoothness, ass:kernel-regularity, ass:iid-sampling, ass:kernel-bandwidth-consistency, ass:oracle-transform-lambda-regularity, def:dinkelbach-transform, def:regular-root-objects, and def:dr-endpoint-handle, the oracle known-population-nuisance transform satisfies sup_{lambda in Lambda_sigma}|hat_Phi^or_{sigma,n}(lambda)-Phi_sigma(lambda;P)| ->p 0.
statements.proof_tex: Write psi_lambda,n(O)=psi_{sigma,lambda,n}(O;P), Phi(lambda)=Phi_sigma(lambda;P), and hatPhi_n(lambda)=P_n psi_lambda,n. Decompose sup_lambda |hatPhi_n(lambda)-Phi(lambda)| <= sup_lambda |(P_n-P)psi_lambda,n| + sup_lambda |P psi_lambda,n-Phi(lambda)|. First consider the centered empirical term. By ass:oracle-transform-lambda-regularity the class has lambda-continuous versions, a scalar Lipschitz modulus in lambda, envelope |psi_lambda,n(O)| <= C{1+|K((D-d)/h_n)|/h_n}, and variance sup_lambda Var_P(psi_lambda,n)<=C/h_n. Ass:local-dose-density gives f_d(X)>=c_f>0 a.e.; together with ass:bounded-outcome and ass:kernel-regularity this envelope may be written as a measurable dominating envelope G_n(O)=C'(1+|Y|)|K((D-d)/h_n)|/(c_f h_n)+C' with E G_n^2=O(1/h_n). Let M_n be a grid of Lambda_sigma with mesh delta_n=(n h_n)^(-1/2) and cardinality O((n h_n)^(1/2)). At every grid point, Chebyshev's inequality and the variance bound give P(|(P_n-P)psi_lambda,n|>epsilon)<=C/(n h_n epsilon^2). A union bound over the grid gives probability O((n h_n)^(-1/2)) for any fixed epsilon. The scalar Lipschitz modulus makes the empirical and population averages change by at most O(delta_n) between adjacent grid points, hence the grid maximum controls the full supremum. Since ass:kernel-bandwidth-consistency implies n h_n -> infinity, sup_lambda |(P_n-P)psi_lambda,n| ->p 0. Second consider the deterministic localization bias. By def:regular-root-objects, psi_lambda,n=m_{sigma,lambda}(X)+K((D-d)/h_n){G_sigma(O,pi_{sigma,lambda}(X);P)-N_sigma(X,pi_{sigma,lambda}(X))}/(h_n f_d(X)), while def:dinkelbach-transform gives Phi(lambda)=E m_{sigma,lambda}(X). Conditional on X=x, the expectation of the residual term equals integral K(u) r_lambda(d+h_n u,x) f_{d+h_n u}(x)/f_d(x) du, where r_lambda(a,x)=E[G_sigma(O,pi_{sigma,lambda}(x);P)-N_sigma(x,pi_{sigma,lambda}(x)) | D=a,X=x]. The construction in def:regular-root-objects gives r_lambda(d,x)=0. Ass:oracle-transform-lambda-regularity gives continuity at d uniformly in lambda; ass:local-dose-smoothness and ass:local-dose-density bound f_{d+h_n u}(x)/f_d(x) on the compact support of K for all large n; and ass:bounded-outcome bounds the residual. Dominated convergence with ass:kernel-regularity therefore yields sup_{lambda in Lambda_sigma}|P psi_lambda,n-Phi(lambda)| -> 0. Combining the centered and deterministic terms proves the uniform transform ULLN.

sampling_model.design: i.i.d. sampling from the observed law P of O = (Y,S,D,X) under the Lee-Liu sufficient-set model at a fixed interior dose d
sampling_model.units: individual observations with pretreatment covariates X and outcomes observed only when S=1
sampling_model.treatment: continuous or multivalued treatment D, localized at d with kernel K and bandwidth h_n for estimation
sampling_model.overlap: strict selected-mass overlap on the Lee-Liu Frechet band together with positive conditional treatment density f_d(X) at the target dose
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: cross-fitted kernel-localized estimation of s_d, Psi_d^L, Psi_d^U, underline_pi_d, and overline_pi_d, combined with an orthogonal correction inside the transformed endpoint objective, explicit finite-bandwidth bias control via local dose smoothness, and undersmoothing so the O(h_n^2) kernel bias is second order

estimand_functional: Theta_d(P) = [ inf_{pi in Pi_d(P)} E[Psi_d^L(X,pi(X))] / E[pi(X)], sup_{pi in Pi_d(P)} E[Psi_d^U(X,pi(X))] / E[pi(X)] ]

bibliography.citation: Lee, D. S. (2009). Training, Wages, and Sample Selection: Estimating Sharp Bounds on Treatment Effects. Review of Economic Studies 76(3): 1071-1102.
Lee, Y.-Y., and C.-A. Liu (2025). Lee Bounds with a Continuous Treatment in Sample Selection. arXiv:2411.04312.
Semenova, V. (2024). Generalized Lee Bounds. Journal of Econometrics, forthcoming; arXiv:2008.12720.
Jonzon, G., E. E. Gabriel, A. Sjolander, and M. C. Sachs (2025). Adding covariates to bounds: What is the question? arXiv:2502.03156.
Dinkelbach, W. (1967). On Nonlinear Fractional Programming. Management Science 13(7): 492-498.
Ben-Michael, E. (2025). Partial identification via conditional linear programs: estimation and policy learning. arXiv:2506.12215.
Colangelo, K., and Y.-Y. Lee (2025). Double Debiased Machine Learning Nonparametric Inference with Continuous Treatments. arXiv:2004.03036.
Fang, Z., and A. Santos (2019). Inference on Directionally Differentiable Functions. Review of Economic Studies 86(1): 377-412; arXiv:1404.3763.

tldr: The unresolved sufficient-set case in Lee and Liu's continuous-treatment Lee bounds is a law-level aggregation problem, not a pointwise trimming problem. For a fixed dose d, the always-takers average dose-response function is interval identified because the sufficient-set restriction implies an observable Frechet band [underline_pi_d(X), overline_pi_d(X)] for the conditional always-taker mass, while the endpoint averages are ratios with the common denominator E[pi(X)]. The proposal characterizes each sharp endpoint as the value of a global support-function problem over measurable selector functions pi in Pi_d(P), reduces that linear-fractional program to a scalar Dinkelbach root, and isolates a root-normalized common-threshold condition under which the naive pointwise Frechet plug-in is exactly sharp. A kernel-localized doubly robust endpoint handle then targets the smooth-root submodel where the finite-bandwidth transformed score equals the population transform up to an explicit O(h_n^2) bias under local dose smoothness, the root derivative is fixed at -E[pi_sigma^*(X)], and honest coverage at tie and boundary laws remains the explicit open question.

project_justification.gap: LeeLiu2025ContinuousLee identifies the continuous-treatment always-takers ADRF under sufficient values, but in the sufficient-set covariate case the conditional always-taker share is only constrained by an observable Frechet band and the paper leaves the covariate-aggregated sharp identified set unresolved.
project_justification.niche: The missing object is a globally compatible selector process, not another conditional trimming formula. JonzonGabrielSjolanderSachs2025CovariatesBounds makes clear that pointwise sharp conditional bounds need not aggregate to a uniformly sharp marginal bound, and BenMichael2025ConditionalLP does not cover the denominator-coupled ratio structure here.
project_justification.fill: The project fills that gap with a measurable-selector support-function characterization, a scalar Dinkelbach threshold representation, a primitive collapse criterion for exact pointwise plug-in sharpness, and a regular-regime endpoint estimator whose expansion keeps the finite-bandwidth kernel bias explicit and uses the h_n-scaled variance normalization implied by local dose localization.

related_work: LeeLiu2025ContinuousLee is the direct anchor: it extends Lee bounds to continuous or multivalued treatments and leaves the sufficient-set covariate case with only conditional Frechet information on the always-taker share. Lee2009SharpBounds is the binary-treatment benchmark showing how sharp trimming follows once the always-taker share is identified. Semenova2024GeneralizedLee provides the closest support-function and orthogonal-moment template for Lee-type bounds with covariates, but there the relevant always-taker covariate law is identified rather than chosen from a denominator-coupled band. JonzonGabrielSjolanderSachs2025CovariatesBounds formalizes the pointwise-versus-uniform sharpness distinction that drives the present obstruction. BenMichael2025ConditionalLP gives de-biased inference for bounds that are expectations of conditional linear-program optima, but the present target is a global linear-fractional program over measurable selectors with one shared denominator. ColangeloLee2025ContinuousDML supplies the local-dose kernel localization, orthogonalization, and bias-control template for continuous-treatment ADRF inference before the nonsmooth support-function step. FangSantos2019DirectionalInference is the natural template once tie and boundary laws make the Dinkelbach root map only directionally differentiable.
honest_scope: The identification kernel is the global support-function and common-threshold collapse characterization. The inferential proposition is limited to the smooth-root interior-curvature submodel, where local treatment density, local smoothness in the treatment coordinate, undersmoothing, and explicit finite-bandwidth bias control support an h_n-scaled Gaussian limit. Honest coverage when the selector ties or hits a Frechet boundary remains the explicit open question. The sufficient-value reduction is included as a non-novel sanity check.
